items.title: 2 years ago a boy died of rabies from a bat. Doctors are reminding the public to be aware
items.summary_auto: In the latest issue of the Canadian Medical Association Journal, infectious disease physicians described the case of an 11-year-old child who died of rabies almost two years ago, and urged people to seek medical attention if they have any contact with bats to prevent potential rabies infection.

items.title: Russian strikes in Ukraine kill at least 8, injure 29
items.summary_auto: Russian missile and drone attacks in the cities of Dnipro and Zaporizhzhia in southeastern Ukraine killed eight people and ‌wounded dozens, Ukrainian authorities said on Monday.

items.title: Australia proposes increasing fines for social media companies in under-16 ban
items.summary_auto: Australia introduced new legislation in Parliament on Monday to strengthen enforcement of its under-16 ‌social media ban and give its internet regulator more power to pursue tech giants in court for non-compliance.

items.title: Canadian tries to set world record for most trees planted in 24 hours — again
items.summary_auto: The last time Kenny Chaplin broke the Guinness World Record for most trees planted in 24 hours, the year was 2001 and he was a much younger man. He's doing it all again in 2026 at the village of Buena Vista, Sask.

items.title: Top physicians warned Winnipeg hospitals nearing 'disintegration' in March letter
items.summary_auto: Top Winnipeg doctors warned in a confidential letter in March that urgent-care centres might have to close and another patient death was inevitable unless immediate changes were made.

items.title: What’s fueling residential school denialism?
items.summary_auto: The senate voted against criminalizing residential school denialism. We look at the debate over how to confront the issue.

items.title: New research suggests invasive spotted lanternfly may be able to survive Canadian winters
items.summary_auto: Research out of the Great Lakes Forestry Centre in Sault Ste. Marie, Ont., is raising new concerns about an invasive insect that has been steadily moving closer to the Canadian border.

items.title: Family of teen fatally injured on Quebec ski lift grieves a life that 'shouldn't be over'
'Feels like checkmate': Cubans chart risky path forward under U.S. pressure
items.summary_auto: Six months into a tumultuous year for Cuba, the winds of change are aggressively blowing across the Straits of Florida, toward the island nation.

items.title: Ottawa set to relaunch federal green home retrofit program in 4 provinces
items.summary_auto: After a hiatus, the popular greener homes  program is returning to Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Island, Quebec and British Columbia, CBC News has learned.

items.title: A key CUSMA 'checkpoint' is coming. Businesses and unions are balancing anxiety and hope
items.summary_auto: Businesses and labour groups are watching with a mix of anxiety and hope as a key trade moment arrives on Wednesday, as Premier Doug Ford sets off on his third trip in less than a month to meet with U.S. lawmakers.

items.title: Apartments can become sweltering in summer. Why heat bylaws could be coming to a city near you
items.summary_auto: As temperatures and deaths connected to heat keep climbing, more tenants, politicians and climate advocates are calling for cooling rules. During municipal debates — from councils in British Columbia to Newfoundland — the same question keeps coming up: Who should pay for the upgrades?

items.title: Some Venezuelans fear a dark and dangerous future in aftermath of twin earthquakes
items.summary_auto: The future seems dark and dangerous for some Venezuelans amid the dust, debris and death shrouding parts of the country that suffered the brunt of twin earthquakes, creating one of the worst natural disasters in its history.

items.title: As winds of change blow toward Cuba, how will Havana chart a path forward?
items.summary_auto: Six months into a tumultuous year for Cuba, the winds of change are aggressively blowing across the Straits of Florida, toward the island nation.

items.title: He built a 20-foot sculpture out of sand | The Moment
items.summary_auto: P.E.I. sculptor Abe Waterman tells The National about the moment he won the Hampton Beach Master Sand Sculpting Classic for his 20-foot-tall sand sculpture titled Hieght of Stupitidy.

items.title: Venezuelans angry over lack of earthquake preparedness
items.summary_auto: As crews continue to search through the rubble for potential survivors, many Venezuelans expressed anger at their government for its lack of disaster preparedness after years of political corruption.

items.title: Residents of Fort Simpson, N.W.T., ordered to evacuate
items.summary_auto: An evacuation order was called around 7 p.m. MT Sunday due to a nearby wildfire. A fire information officer told CBC News the fire is not expected to reach the community overnight. Residents are advised to head to Yellowknife.

items.title: 6 hospitalized after packed Montreal Jazz Festival concert, officials say response went as planned
items.summary_auto: Though six people were taken to hospital during a free Montreal Jazz Festival concert on Saturday, organizers and Urgences-santé said the event unfolded as well as hoped for.

items.title: 6 still missing, 4 rescued after charter boat sinks in B.C.'s Georgia Strait
items.summary_auto: Four people have been rescued but a search continues for six people missing after a charter boat sank in British Columbia's Georgia Strait on Sunday.

items.title: Northern Manitoba evacuations continue Sunday as wildfire threat grows
items.summary_auto: Hundreds of people have been forced out of their homes as wildfires continue to threaten communities in northern Manitoba.

items.title: Wildfires prompt First Nation in northwestern Ontario to order vulnerable residents to leave
items.summary_auto: The remote fly-in community of Kasabonika Lake First Nation in northwestern Ontario is making preparations to evacuate vulnerable residents, as concern rises over numerous wildfires surrounding the community. Close to 60 wildfires are now burning across northwestern Ontario after dozens of new fires were sparked over the weekend.

items.title: France reports 1,000 additional deaths as extreme heat shatters European records
items.summary_auto: France saw around 1,000 additional deaths last week at the height of its record-smashing heat wave, the country's public health agency said Sunday, as the head of the World Health Organization (WHO) warned that Europe is now the fastest-warming continent and needs to do more to protect its citizens.
